text: The static structure and dynamic configuration of reaction centers are the core factors that determine the catalytic performance of the catalyst. To this end, it is still a great challenge to utilize the dynamic regulation of catalyst performance. In this work, a simple light-activation method using a TiO2 supported Cu catalyst to regulate the dynamic structural change of Cu active sites during low temperature methanol steam reforming (MSR) was investigated. It was found that the catalyst would be inactivated under light off condition due to the formation of inactive Cu2O from the reduction of Cu metal under dark condition at low temperature. Under UV-LED light activation, the photo-generated charge carriers on the TiO2 surface would instantly reduce the inactive Cu2O to Cu metal, which significantly enhanced the catalytic activity and stability of the catalyst. Under the low intensity solar light, the optimized Cu/TiO2 catalyst showed as high as 1724.1 μmol g⁻¹ min⁻¹ H2 evolution rate, outperforming most of the reported photocatalytic and thermocatalytic activities. Moreover, by means of the strong light-matter-reactant interaction, the Cu active sites can be regulated in situ and in real time, realizing the functionalization of catalysts at their peak performance.
